event: The Lamentation over the Dead Christ
symbols: dead body of Christ — the sacrificial Lamb slain for the sins of the world
blue mantle of Mary — purity and heavenly sorrow of the Mother of God
wound marks on Christ's body — the price of redemption (Isaiah 53:5)
white linen cloth — burial shroud prefiguring the resurrection wrappings
X-shaped cross (left wing) — attribute of Saint Andrew, patron of the male donors
dove (right wing) — Holy Spirit, associated with the Annunciation to the Virgin
kneeling donors — human participation in Christ's sacrifice through prayerful devotion
triptych format — Trinitarian structure and liturgical opening/closing for sacred contemplation
tears and grief of mourners — the Church's sorrow that gives way to Easter hope
description: A three-paneled triptych with an elaborate gilt frame. The central panel shows the dead Christ laid across the lap and arms of the Virgin Mary in a blue mantle over a white head covering. Several mourning figures surround them: a woman with loose reddish hair bends close, another supports Christ's arm, and male figures stand behind in grief. The left wing shows male donors in dark clothing kneeling in prayer, with an upper scene of Saint Andrew beside an X-shaped cross.
theological_description: The Haneton Triptych presents the Lamentation -- the moment between Christ's death on the cross and His entombment -- as the theological nadir of Holy Saturday, when the Word made flesh lies lifeless in His mother's arms. This is the fulfillment of Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce Mary's soul (Luke 2:35). The Virgin's grief is not merely maternal but ecclesial: she represents the Church holding the broken body of her Lord before the dawn of resurrection. Christ's visible wounds (the pierced side from John 19:34, the nail marks) are displayed not as mere suffering but as the instruments of redemption -- 'by his wounds we are healed' (Isaiah 53:5). The donor panels integrate the Haneton family into this salvific drama through intercessory prayer, reflecting the medieval theology that the faithful participate mystically in Christ's Passion. Saint Andrew's cross on the left wing and the Annunciation scene with the dove on the right wing bracket the central Lamentation between apostolic witness and the Incarnation, framing Christ's death within the entire arc of salvation history. The triptych form itself is liturgical: when opened, it reveals the mystery of redemption; when closed, it veils it -- mirroring the hiddenness of Holy Saturday before Easter's revelation.

scripture_refs.reason: The taking down of Jesus's body by Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus -- the narrative moment preceding this lamentation
Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce Mary's soul -- fulfilled in her grief over her dead Son
The Suffering Servant wounded for our transgressions -- the theological foundation of the depicted scene
Mary and the beloved disciple at the cross -- the same group now gathered around the dead Christ
The piercing of Christ's side with a spear -- the wound visible on the body
Matthew's account of the burial preparation by Joseph of Arimathea
Messianic psalm describing pierced hands and feet -- the visible wounds on the dead Christ
Redeemed by the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish
